quick_facts.label: Related occupational standard
quick_facts.value: NSDC lists “Front Office Executive” under Front Office Management as qualification pack THC/Q0109 at NSQF Level 5.

quick_facts.label: Comparable NCS occupation
quick_facts.value: The National Career Service (NCS) maps hotel reception work to NCO 2015 code 4224.0100, “Receptionist (Hotel),” and includes “Front Office Executive” among its keywords.

quick_facts.label: Short-course entry benchmark
quick_facts.value: Ministry of Tourism HSRT guidance specifies a Front Office Associate course mapped to THC/Q0102: 12th-class pass, 580 total training hours (340 institute hours plus 240 hours of OJT), and 60% minimum for certification. This is a course-specific benchmark, not a universal hiring rule.

quick_facts.label: Typical setting
quick_facts.value: Hotel front-office work is a guest-facing tourism-and-hospitality role; NCS names hotels and restaurants as probable employers.

sections.heading: Core responsibilities
paragraphs: A Front Office Executive is the hotel’s reception and guest-service point of contact. In practice, the role covers responding to calls and guest enquiries; checking room availability and assigning accommodation; recording guest identity, arrival and expected departure; keeping occupancy and reservation records current; coordinating guest-arrival information with housekeeping, dining and kitchen teams; and supporting billing and collections.

At a branded-property level, duties can also include verifying identity and payment at check-in, issuing keys, creating guest accounts, processing payments and charges, reconciling cashiering reports, providing directions and property information, and following up on guest requests.
bullets: Handle reservations, arrivals, departures, room allocation and guest records.
Respond to guest queries, telephone calls and service requests with timely coordination across hotel departments.
Support accurate billing, payment handling, shift reports and receipt controls.
Maintain professional guest communication, service standards, confidentiality and safety procedures.

sections.heading: Entry routes in India
paragraphs: The listed parent programmes—B.Sc. in Hospitality and Hotel Administration, BBA in Hotel Management and Diploma in Hotel Management—are relevant preparation routes because they provide broader hospitality education before entry to hotel guest-services roles. NCS describes a graduate degree as preferable for the comparable hotel receptionist occupation, rather than as an absolute requirement.

A shorter skills-first route is also available in the Ministry of Tourism’s HSRT framework: the Front Office Associate course requires a 12th-class pass and includes both institute training and on-the-job training. Separately, NSDC lists Front Office Executive (THC/Q0109) at NSQF Level 5; candidates should confirm the current assessment, provider and eligibility details directly with the training provider or awarding body before enrolling.

Requirements differ by employer and seniority. For example, a Marriott Front Office Executive posting in Delhi NCR stated a high-school-equivalent education and at least one year each of related and supervisory experience, illustrating that individual vacancies can set requirements beyond a course credential.
bullets: Degree route: hospitality or hotel-management programmes, followed by hotel internships and entry-level front-office applications.
Diploma route: hotel-management diploma plus practical training or internship experience.
Skills route: Front Office Associate training under the Ministry of Tourism HSRT framework, subject to course availability and provider eligibility.
Direct-employment route: apply to hotel reception or guest-service vacancies where the employer’s stated education, language and experience criteria are met.

sections.heading: Skills to build
paragraphs: The role combines service communication with reliable operational administration. NCS identifies Microsoft Office, database familiarity, organisation, troubleshooting while meeting customer requirements, knowledge of local tourist destinations and the ability to put people at ease as relevant competencies. It also identifies independent working, problem-solving, interpersonal ability and communication as desirable.

Hotel employers may additionally expect accurate written and spoken communication, telephone etiquette, use of computers and POS systems, proper cash and payment controls, and collaboration with other departments. Candidates benefit from practising these skills in a hotel internship, simulated front-office lab or guest-service role.
bullets: Clear guest communication and telephone etiquette.
Empathy, problem-solving and calm handling of requests or complaints.
Reservation, occupancy, billing and payment-record accuracy.
Computer, database and POS-system confidence.
Interdepartmental coordination with housekeeping, food and beverage, security and accounts.
Knowledge of the local area and visitor services.

sections.heading: Workplace and schedule
paragraphs: Front Office Executives work primarily at hotel reception desks and front-office areas, with regular coordination across operational departments. NCS notes that the comparable hotel receptionist occupation is not a work-from-home role and that shift systems and overtime may be available because establishments operate continuously.

The work is customer-facing and may require standing or walking for extended periods. A current employer posting also indicates use of computers/POS systems and adherence to appearance, safety, confidentiality and service-quality standards.
bullets: Usually on-site at a hotel, resort or similar hospitality property.
Shift work may include early, late, overnight, weekend and holiday coverage depending on property operations.
Regular contact with guests and internal service teams; accuracy and professional presentation matter.
Physical demands can include extended standing or walking.

sections.heading: Progression
paragraphs: A practical progression begins in reception, guest service or front-office executive work and develops toward supervisory and management responsibilities as the person gains competence in guest recovery, cashiering, reservations, shift control and team coordination. NCS gives an indicative comparable path of Receptionist (Hotel) to Reporting Head to Administration Supervisor to Customer Service Manager; it expressly notes that progression is indicative.

A second NCS tourism-and-hospitality career profile describes the more front-office-specific sequence Customer Service Executive (Meet and Greet) to Front Desk Supervisor to Front Desk Manager. Titles, timelines and promotion criteria vary substantially by hotel size, brand, property segment and demonstrated experience.
bullets: Front Office Executive / Receptionist / Guest Service Associate.
Senior executive or shift-focused responsibilities.
Front Desk Supervisor or comparable supervisory role.
Front Desk Manager, customer-service manager or wider front-office management roles.

sections.heading: Compensation context
paragraphs: Salary is highly variable by city, hotel category, shift pattern, language skills, prior experience, incentives and benefits. As dated reference context only, NCS’s comparable Receptionist (Hotel) page—last updated December 29, 2023—listed ₹12,500–₹25,000 per month for candidates with experience and explicitly described the figures as indicative and subject to change. It should not be treated as a current market quote or salary guarantee.

Before accepting an offer, compare fixed pay with the full terms: service charge or incentives where applicable, meals, accommodation or transport, uniform costs, statutory deductions, weekly off, shift/overnight allowance and probation conditions.
bullets: Use vacancy-specific written compensation details rather than generic online salary estimates.
Compare annual fixed pay and take-home pay after deductions.
Ask whether the role includes service charge, food, transport, accommodation, shift allowance or other benefits.
Confirm working hours, roster, overtime policy and probation terms in writing.
